# The Mental Model of Git

Most people who find Git confusing are missing one picture, not a
hundred commands. Once you can see what Git is actually doing, the
commands stop being magic incantations and become obvious moves on a
board you understand. This chapter draws that board. There are only
three ideas on it: a commit is a snapshot of your whole project, your
work lives in three places at once, and a branch is just a movable
label. Everything in the rest of the book is built on these three.

We will keep remotes (GitHub, the shared copy) out of the picture
until Chapter 9. You can do real, version-controlled research for
months without ever touching a remote, and mixing it in now is exactly
what makes Git feel overwhelming. Learn the local model first.

![The mental model of Git: a change moves across the three trees by `git add` and `git commit` (and back with `git restore`), and the repository stores a chain of commits, each a whole-project snapshot, with `main` as a movable pointer and `HEAD` pointing at it. The remote is deferred to Chapter 9.](../assets/diagrams/fig-git-mental-model){#fig-git-mental-model fig-alt="A two-band diagram. The top band, labelled the three trees and the daily loop, shows four cards left to right: Working tree (files on disk), Staging area (the index), Repository (committed history), and a dashed Remote (a shared copy on GitHub). Solid arrows labelled git add and git commit run left to right; a dashed amber arrow labelled git restore curves back from the repository to the working tree; a dashed arrow labelled push slash fetch links the repository to the remote. The bottom band, labelled what the repository stores, shows four commits in a chain (20c7835, 758175e, f9a193f, dcb18de) joined by arrows, with pointers HEAD and main both aimed at the newest commit." width="6in"}

Refer back to @fig-git-mental-model as you read; each section below is
one part of it.

## A commit is a snapshot of the whole project

The single most useful correction to most beginners' intuition: a
commit is not a record of what changed in one file. A commit is a
complete snapshot of every tracked file in your project at one moment,
plus a label saying who took it, when, and why. Git is not storing a
stack of diffs; it is storing a sequence of photographs of the entire
working directory.

You can see this directly. Git stores each commit as a small object,
and `git cat-file -p` prints it in plain text. Here is the newest
commit in the running example:

```bash
$ git cat-file -p HEAD
tree 5c2b271dba406fa5181d962342644d8a7799d0b2
parent f9a193f0ac3e4535babbb484bf65feecba5d2582
author Pat Researcher <pat@example.edu> 1780570800 +0000
committer Pat Researcher <pat@example.edu> 1780570800 +0000

Note headline result in README
```

A commit points at exactly two interesting things. The `parent` is the
commit that came before it, which is how history chains together. The
`tree` is the snapshot itself: a listing of the whole project at that
moment. Follow the tree and you get the project, top level first:

```bash
$ git cat-file -p HEAD^{tree}
100644 blob b22a497d8a12ece1c64d20db595f9a0c98e7c057	.gitignore
100644 blob cc7f10a5617b488417a5230dcf02829134d4e1a4	README.md
040000 tree c95f734379adfe83a1e45ac4ada5468fc1700f0d	output
100644 blob b7c5ea44aca071822375d6a62410950f654a129c	report.qmd
040000 tree 7790e6d9f8d467334be13c42605f44b8cdc49147	scripts
```

Every file is a `blob` (its contents) and every subfolder is another
`tree`. The commit captures all of it. This is why checking out an old
commit restores the entire project to how it was, not just one file,
and why Git can never "lose half your work" inside a commit: a commit
is all-or-nothing by construction.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Snapshots are what make Git an honest scientific record. A committed
result is tied to the exact state of every script that produced it. If
your `git log` says the main table came from commit `f9a193f`, you can
return the entire project to `f9a193f` and rerun it. That is the
difference between "the figure in the paper" and "a figure I can
regenerate from a known, labelled state of the code."
:::

Two more facts about commits, both visible above. The long string after
`HEAD` is the commit's **hash**, a fingerprint computed from its
contents; change anything and the hash changes, which is why hashes
differ across machines and why they cannot be quietly forged. And every
commit names its parent, so the history is a chain: each snapshot knows
the one before it.

## Your work lives in three trees

At any moment, a tracked file in your project exists in up to three
versions at once. Git calls these the three trees, and they are the
left half of @fig-git-mental-model.

The **working tree** is the set of real files on your disk: what your
editor opens, what your scripts read. This is the only one of the three
you edit directly.

The **staging area**, also called the index, is a holding pen for the
next commit. Nothing you change reaches history until you place it here
with `git add`. The staging area is the feature beginners most often
overlook and advanced users most rely on: it lets you commit a clean,
deliberate subset of your changes instead of whatever happens to be on
disk.

The **repository** is the permanent history of commits, the snapshots
described above. `git commit` takes whatever is in the staging area and
writes it as a new snapshot here.

So the daily loop from Chapter 5 is just moving a change rightward
across @fig-git-mental-model: edit in the working tree, `git add` it
into the staging area, `git commit` it into the repository. And the
undo commands move leftward: `git restore` discards a working-tree
edit, `git restore --staged` pulls a change back out of the staging
area. The same three trees explain both the saving and the undoing.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Because a commit snapshots the staging area, not your disk, it is
possible to commit something different from what you see in your
editor. If you edit a file after `git add` and then commit without
adding again, the commit captures the earlier, staged version. This is
not a bug; it is the staging area doing its job. The habit that keeps
you safe is the one from Chapter 5: run `git status` and `git diff
--staged` before you commit, so you commit what you mean to.
:::

## A branch is a movable pointer

The word "branch" makes people imagine something heavy, like a separate
copy of the project. It is the opposite. A branch is a tiny, movable
label that points at one commit. That is all.

The running example has one branch, `main`:

```bash
$ git branch
* main
```

And `HEAD` is simply the pointer that tracks where you are now. In the
normal case it does not point straight at a commit; it points at a
branch, which in turn points at a commit:

```bash
$ cat .git/HEAD
ref: refs/heads/main
```

So the chain is `HEAD` to `main` to the newest commit, exactly as drawn
on the right of @fig-git-mental-model. When you make a new commit, Git
writes the snapshot, then slides the `main` pointer forward to it, and
`HEAD` comes along because it was pointing at `main`. The history
itself does not move; the label does. Seen as a chain of commits, the
example is perfectly linear:

```bash
$ git log --graph --oneline
* dcb18de Note headline result in README
* f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
* 758175e Add polars cleaning script
* 20c7835 Set up project skeleton and data generator
```

Because a branch is only a pointer, creating one is instant and free:
you are not copying files, you are writing down one more label. That is
the whole reason branching in Git is cheap enough to do constantly,
which Chapter 7 builds on. For now the point is conceptual: "switch
branches" means "move the `HEAD` label and update the working tree to
match," not "open a different folder."

## Local now, remote later

Everything above happens entirely on your own machine. The repository,
the staging area, the branches, the whole history: all local, all
usable on a laptop with no internet. A **remote** is just a second copy
of the repository living somewhere else, usually on GitHub, that you
synchronise with using `git push` and `git fetch`. It is drawn faded
and dashed on the right of @fig-git-mental-model precisely because it is
not part of the core local model and not part of this chapter. When you
are ready to share, collaborate, or back up your work, Chapter 9
introduces remotes properly. Until then, the three trees and the branch
pointers are the complete picture.

## What you can now do

You can read a commit as a snapshot of the whole project, name the
three trees and say which command moves a change between them, and
explain `main` and `HEAD` as movable pointers rather than copies. This
is the model the rest of the book assumes. When a later command seems
confusing, come back to @fig-git-mental-model and ask the only three
questions that matter: which tree is this change in, which commit does
the snapshot capture, and where is the branch pointer now.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Working in your clone of the running example:

1. Run `git cat-file -p HEAD`. Identify the `tree` line and the
   `parent` line, and say in one sentence what each one points to.
2. Run `git cat-file -p HEAD^{tree}`. Confirm that the entries match
   the files and folders at the top level of the project. Why is this
   the right notion of a "snapshot" for reproducibility?
3. Run `cat .git/HEAD`, then `git branch`. Trace the pointer chain from
   `HEAD` to a specific commit hash, naming each hop.
4. Make and commit one trivial change, then run `git log --oneline`
   before and after. Which pointer moved, and which commits kept their
   hashes? Tie your answer back to @fig-git-mental-model.
